answer:
The choice of a single English word for dharma would break down most painfully at Yājñavalkya Smṛti 1.3–1.9, where within eight verses dharma crosses epistemology, ethics, soteriology, and legal authority — no single word survives that traverse. The three key passages, one from each text, show where each candidate term picks up only one corner:

---

**1. 'Duty' — Manusmṛti 1.81–82 (ch. 1, p-fcafde / p-1365cd)**

"In the Krita age, Dharma is four-footed and complete, and so is truth; no gain comes to men through any unrighteousness. In the other ages, Dharma is successively deprived of one foot due to what is gained; and through theft, falsehood, and deceit, Dharma diminishes by one foot in each."

'Duty' picks up the obligatory dimension of dharma elsewhere in Manu (each varṇa has its assigned tasks), but here it collapses. Dharma is a cosmic, ambulatory entity that walks on four legs in the Golden Age and progressively loses limbs as the world declines. No individual or institution has a 'duty' to maintain a four-footed abstraction; the text is describing a metaphysical condition of the universe itself. 'Duty' implies a subject with an obligation; this passage has dharma as the subject — it is the agent being depleted. Manu 1.86 deepens the problem: "In the Krita age, the highest Dharma is tapas; in the Treta, knowledge; in the Dvapara, yajña; in the Kali age, giving is the one Dharma." If dharma = duty, you must explain why a duty mutates across cosmic ages — which duty-language has no apparatus to do.

---

**2. 'Law' — Yājñavalkya Smṛti 1.8 (ch. 1, p-1e45c4)**

"Of sacrifice, right conduct, sense-control, non-violence, giving, and the study of the Veda — of these actions, this is the highest dharma: the vision of the Self through yoga."

'Law' picks up the prescriptive-normative dimension of dharma — Yājñavalkya is ranking practices in order of authority and binding force. But no enacted legal code can have 'vision of the Self through yoga' (ātmadarśana via yoga) as its supreme command. The text is structured as a soteriological hierarchy, not a legal code: the practices listed are ranked toward moksha, and the apex is a mystical telos, not a statutory obligation. A translator who renders dharma as 'law' at Yj_1.1 ("Declare to us the law of the varṇas") must then write at Yj_1.8: "This is the highest law: the vision of the Self through yoga" — which is either incoherent as jurisprudence or evacuates 'law' of all its normal meaning. The identical word dharma simultaneously means the legal authority of the Dharmaśāstra (Yj_1.3) and a liberation-goal that no court can mandate (Yj_1.8).

---

**3. 'Virtue' — Viṣṇu Smṛti 1.5 (ch. 1, p-8081a1)**

"The glorious one, composed of dharma (sacred law) and truth, honored for his strides and valor, Whose great snout was penance, whose knees were the sacrificial animal, of mighty form."

'Virtue' picks up the moral-excellence dimension of dharma — Vishnu is supremely righteous. But the passage is a somatological allegory: every part of the Cosmic Boar's body is a component of the Vedic sacrifice (mouth = fire-altar, tongue = sacrificial fire, bristles = darbha grass, etc.), and the body as a whole is said to be composed of dharma and truth. Being 'composed of virtue' would suggest character-psychology — good qualities a person has. The text means something structurally different: dharma here is the ritual-cosmic order that Vishnu's body literally instantiates and of which it is made. Dharma is not a quality he possesses; it is the ontological material of his form. And Viṣṇu 1.54 compounds this: "O knower of dharma and adharma, O embodiment of dharma, O source of dharma" — dharma is simultaneously an object of knowledge, an ontological state one can embody, and a causal origin from which others derive dharma. No single virtue-concept spans knower → embodier → source.

---

**Summary of where the choice collapses most painfully:**

Yājñavalkya 1.3–1.9 is the single most dangerous zone. Within eight verses, dharma is: a body of knowledge (epistemology: Vedas + Nyāya + Mīmāṃsā, Yj_1.3), a mark of right gifting practice (Yj_1.6), the multi-rooted norm grounded in scripture, tradition, virtuous conduct, self-pleasure, and right resolve (Yj_1.7), the soteriological apex of all practice = Self-vision through yoga (Yj_1.8), and what qualified assemblies authoritatively declare (juridical: Yj_1.9). A translator who commits to 'law' at Yj_1.1 must render Yj_1.8 as "the highest law is yogic Self-vision" — a sentence that breaks law-language. A translator who commits to 'virtue' must render Yj_1.3 as "the Vedas and Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā are sources of virtue" — which is not what Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā are. A translator who commits to 'duty' must render Yj_1.9 as "what qualified assemblies declare is duty" — but the text says what they declare IS dharma itself, not merely obligatory.

tool_calls.name: list_works
tool_calls.result_summary: Found all 38 works; confirmed Manusmṛti (unknown-manusmrti-347b76), Yājñavalkya Smṛti (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6), Viṣṇu Smṛti (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8) all present

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Full 119-paragraph chapter; found dharma as cosmic four-footed entity (1.81-82), age-specific highest practice (1.86), varna-duties (1.87-91), Brahmin as incarnation of dharma (1.98-99), and multiple passages using law/duty/righteousness interchangeably

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: 66-paragraph chapter; found dharma as cosmic substance of Boar avatar body (Vi_1.5), dharma of varṇas/āśramas as Earth's support (Vi_1.47-48), and triple invocation: knower/embodiment/source of dharma (Vi_1.54)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Opening verses: dharma of varṇas (Yj_1.1), dharma's 14 sources including Nyāya/Mīmāṃsā/Vedas (Yj_1.3), dharma mark of right gifting (Yj_1.6), multi-rooted dharma (Yj_1.7), highest dharma = yogic Self-vision (Yj_1.8), dharma = what qualified assemblies declare (Yj_1.9)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Chapter on Legal Procedure: dharma = juridical qualification of court members (Yj_2.2-3); dharma = knowing smṛti + moral virtues (truthfulness, impartiality) — confirms law/virtue conflation in same word
